other_observers: Many, including Jim Schmoker, Tony Leukering, Rachel Hopper, Doug Faulkner, Larry Semo, Ric Olson, Warren Finch, Dick Schottler, Bob Spencer, Mark Peterson, Bill Maynard, Dick Maxfield, Joe Himmel, Loch Kilpatrick, and others Im forgetting or didnt k; Found that morning by Mike Henwood
description: A tiny gray and white waterbird. Completely dwarfed by the Gadwalls it was hanging out with. Much smaller than even a teal would appear next to the Gadwalls. Very short, bluntly pointed bill that appeared grayish yellow when it caught the sunlight. Forehead, crown, lores, and area immediately behind and under the eye are all nearly black. There were no pale highlights visible in the black portions of the head. The eye was dark and completely surrounded by black plumage. Chin, throat, lower cheeks, and breast almost pure white. Thick spur of white extends up sides of nape and back of head well above the level of the eye. A thin black collar extends down from the lower nape nearly to the waterline but doesnt connect across the breast. The upperparts behind this collar are medium gray, perhaps similar in tone to a typical adult California Gull. The wingtips are also almost black. The flanks of the bird had extensive white, with only minimal gray streaking appearing heavier towards the back half of the bird. These gray streaks trended diagonally upwards and backwards on the flanks of the bird. Overall very compact looking with stocky neck, short tail and short wing projection about even with tail tip.Behaviors: When I first arrived the bird was in flight, moving out farther towards the central eastern part of the reservoir. Shortly thereafter it made another short flight. In flight it stayed low, with fast choppy wingbeats and a direct style of flight. When it landed it skipped three times or so. At this point it joined a group of resting Gadwalls, where it stayed for the rest of my observation. At times it appeared to be sleeping. It preened some and stretched its wings a couple of times. I observed Ring-billed gulls harassing the bird 3 or 4 times- in each case it dove to avoid the gull, pushing itself underwater with its wings.Call: not heardPlumage: juvenile
photographer: Photographer: Bill Schmoker, Bill Schmoker, Bill Schmoker, Bill Schmoker
